design_philosophy.core_loop: One-on-one fighting matches with two-segment structure: first defeat regular-sized monster as a Ranger, then battle giant version of same enemy
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Character matchup and combat execution against escalating monster sizes and difficulty
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Battle as iconic Power Rangers characters in direct combat scenarios mirroring the TV show's monster-of-the-week format
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Commits fully to fighting game format rather than action-platformer like SNES version; each Ranger has distinct moveset; two-segment boss structure creates rhythm of escalation within each encounter

difficulty.checkpointing: Scenario Mode progresses through series of CPU-controlled opponents; each match is discrete encounter
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Character selection offers different fight mechanics; unbalanced difficulty noted by GamePro suggests variable challenge across matchups

progression.upgrade_system: Character unlocks: Green Ranger and Dragonzord become available after defeating them in Scenario Mode
progression.unlock_mechanism: Story progression through defeating opponents unlocks additional playable characters
replayability_hooks: Two game modes (Scenario Mode vs CPU, Battle Mode for two-player competition)
Multiple playable characters with distinct movesets
Green Ranger/Dragonzord unlock provides progression milestone

world_building_techniques: Character roster from TV series provides implicit narrative context
Monster enemies reflect show's antagonist designs
Megazord as final/ultimate playable unit carries show mythology

boss_design.philosophy: Two-tiered escalation: regular-sized monster encounter followed by giant transformation of same enemy creates rhythm within individual battles; mirrors TV show's Megazord transformation sequences
notable_bosses: Green Ranger (unlockable character, encountered in progression)
Megazord (ultimate playable unit and likely final opponent)

faction_indicators: Ranger selection creates implicit team affiliation (original five rangers plus Green Ranger)
danger_signals: Unbalanced difficulty creates potential for frustrating matchups
One-dimensional gameplay noted in reviews suggests limited mechanical depth for extended competitive play
environmental_storytelling: Character-specific movesets visually distinguish Rangers (trademark moves mentioned in SNES reviews apply here)
Monster designs carry visual language from TV show

sources_used: wikipedia - Mighty Morphin Power Rangers (video game)
